sector: Utilities — Independent Power Producer (merchant nuclear + gas/geothermal)
bull_thesis: Constellation Energy is the best-positioned supplier of always-available power to the AI build-out, and the closest thing to a toll booth on the computing capacity the large cloud providers (hyperscalers) are racing to build. The case rests on scarcity that cannot be replicated: 32.4 GW of already-built, carbon-free nuclear baseload, all licensed by the Nuclear Regulatory Commission, that no rival can permit and construct inside a hyperscaler's 2027-2029 window. Two flagship 20-year power-purchase agreements (PPAs, long-term contracts to sell electricity at a set price) show the model repeats at premium economics: Microsoft at the Crane plant (835 MW, ~2028 restart, backed by a $1B Department of Energy loan guarantee, with a federal regulator (FERC) PJM-grid-rule waiver granted 2026-06-01) and Meta at Clinton (1,121 MW, signed 2025-06-03, deliveries beginning June 2027). The chief executive describes a third hyperscaler deal as in the 'late innings.' The $16.4B Calpine acquisition (closed 2026-01-07, adding about $2 per share to earnings) bolts roughly 26 GW of on-demand gas plus the Geysers geothermal assets onto the nuclear fleet, lifting Constellation to about 55 GW and creating a sellable '24/7 firm power' product (carbon-free baseload plus gas peaking) that is exactly what data centers need to stay online. Two earnings engines compound: long-dated PPAs at premium prices, and a tightening PJM capacity market as demand outgrows supply. Management affirmed fiscal-2026 adjusted earnings guidance of $11-12 per share (versus about $9.05-9.45 in fiscal 2025) and beat the first quarter of 2026 (adjusted earnings of $2.74 per share against $2.56 expected) through a demanding integration quarter, which speaks to strong execution. The forward free-cash-flow guide is a step-change: $8.4B combined across 2026-27 and $11.5-13B across 2028-29. A conservative balance sheet (about $5.9B net debt base versus Vistra's roughly $19.6B) funded the deal with room to spare. There is free optionality on small modular and advanced reactors at Clinton. This is a quality compounder riding a multi-year electrification tailwind, not a cyclical bet.
bear_thesis: Constellation is the most thoroughly discovered name in the power-generation group, and the AI-power upside is largely already in the price. It trades at about 25 times forward earnings (49% above the independent-power-producer (IPP) peer median of roughly 17 times) and about 13 times forward enterprise value to EBITDA (earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization), with a forward free-cash-flow yield of only about 3.8% versus Vistra's roughly 7.2% — leaving little margin of safety. The load-bearing catalysts have essentially already happened: Microsoft/Crane, Meta/Clinton and the Calpine close have all occurred and re-rated the stock; what remains is a future-dated cash inflection (2027-2029) that is real but not yet proven in cash. The trailing reality lags the story badly: fiscal-2025 free cash flow was only about $1.3B against a roughly $86B market value (a trailing free-cash-flow yield below 2%), and fiscal-2025 net margin actually fell about 6.8 percentage points to 9.1% on commodity, zero-emission-credit (ZEC) and outage swings — merchant earnings are still lumpy and exposed to weather and outages. The entire case rides the same single shared driver — the durability of AI capital spending — as the rest of the scale-out basket, so its odds of resolving independently are near zero; an air pocket in AI spending or a monetization scare would compress Constellation's premium multiple faster than its asset base. Adding roughly 26 GW of fossil gas dilutes the carbon-free premium and invites a re-rating to a lower gas multiple. The behind-the-meter, co-location regulatory framework (the Talen interconnection-service-agreement precedent) is still unsettled despite the Crane waiver. You are paying a full premium for an already-known story whose cash proof is years away.
sop_bull: $330-345 per share. This values the whole company on its forward power earnings (after Calpine, with the PPAs fully ramped). Apply about 28 times to a mid-point fiscal-2027 normalized adjusted earnings of about $12.5 per share (Calpine fully synergized, Meta/Clinton commenced, capacity-price uplift) for about $350 on earnings power, less a small haircut for premium fatigue. Cross-check on cash: the 2028-29 guide is $11.5-13B of free cash flow; at about a 3.3% free-cash-flow yield (premium, infrastructure-grade asset) the roughly $12B run-rate supports about $365B of enterprise value; netting roughly $16B of post-deal net debt leaves about $110-115 per share of equity (re-based to about 315M shares), or roughly $310-340 per share. Optionality on small modular/advanced reactors at Clinton plus a third hyperscaler PPA add a further unpriced call worth about $15-25 per share. By parts: the nuclear fleet (premium PPA-contracted baseload) is about 70% of value, roughly $230 per share; Calpine gas plus geothermal (the on-demand firm-power complement) about 25%, roughly $85 per share; growth optionality (small modular reactors plus the next PPA) about $25 per share. Bull sum-of-the-parts (valuing each business line separately): about $340 per share.
sop_bear: $200-215 per share. Strip out the premium and value the company on demonstrated, not promised, economics. Re-rate to about 17 times — the IPP peer-median forward multiple — on fiscal-2026 mid-guide adjusted earnings of about $11.5 per share, for about $195 per share. Cross-check on trailing free cash flow (about $1.3B in fiscal 2025): even at a generous 4.5% yield that supports only about $29B of equity (about $92 per share), underscoring how forward-loaded the case is. Value the gas EBITDA at a lower fossil multiple than nuclear (re-rating risk). By parts: nuclear baseload at a de-rated 16-18 times, about $160 per share; Calpine gas at about 7 times EBITDA (a fossil discount), about $45 per share; no credit for the unfired small-modular-reactor or third-PPA optionality. Apply a haircut for AI-spending-durability risk and merchant-margin volatility. Bear sum-of-the-parts: about $205 per share.
sop_breakdown: The base case blends the proven power-earnings franchise with partial — not full — credit for the forward PPA and Calpine ramp. Nuclear fleet (32.4 GW, premium long-dated PPA-contracted plus capacity-market uplift): about $185 per share — the durable, irreplaceable core, valued at about 20-21 times its share of normalized earnings (a deserved premium to the IPP median for licensed scarcity, but below the bull's 28 times). Calpine gas plus Geysers geothermal (about 26 GW of on-demand firm-power complement): about $60 per share at about 8-9 times EBITDA (a blended multiple — above pure fossil, below nuclear, crediting the firm-power synergy). Growth and optionality (the third hyperscaler PPA in the 'late innings' plus the Clinton small-modular-reactor call): about $15 per share, only partly credited, since the PPA flywheel is proven repeatable but each leg is still future-dated. The sum is about $260 per share. That sits about 5% below the roughly $274 spot price — in other words, the market is already paying slightly above a fair, partial-credit sum-of-the-parts, consistent with a 25-times multiple, 49% above peers.
risk_reward: Poor to balanced. With the shares around $274, the move to fair value (about $260) is negative, roughly 5% down; the move to the bull case (about $340) is about 24% up but requires full Calpine synergy, Meta/Clinton commencement, a third PPA, and sustained AI spending — everything going right and staying right. The downside to the bear case (about $205) is about 25% on an AI-spending digestion or premium-compression event. So at today's price the trade-off is roughly even to slightly negative (24% up against 25% down, with the base case already below the current price). The attractive entry is on a sell-off toward the $205-230 zone, not here.
catalyst_status: FIRED. The three load-bearing catalysts have all occurred and re-rated the stock: (1) Calpine acquisition CLOSED 2026-01-07 ($16.4B, ~$2/sh accretive); (2) Meta/Clinton 20-year PPA SIGNED 2025-06-03 (1,121 MW, commences June 2027); (3) Microsoft/Crane restart de-risked — $1B DOE loan guarantee (Nov 2025) + FERC PJM-rule waiver granted 2026-06-01 (restart still future-dated to ~2028). Remaining live items are SOFT_EXTENDED: a third hyperscaler PPA the CEO called 'late innings' (undated, unnamed, single-binary) and Clinton SMR/advanced-reactor optionality (only 'evaluating regulatory pathways'; ESP expires 2027-03-15, no capital committed). Net: the re-rate is largely spent; what remains is execution/commencement of already-announced deals plus one unverifiable pending award. Defensive/arb profile, not asymmetric special-sit.
peer_comps_note: The true peers are the merchant-power and nuclear independent-power-producer (IPP) scale-out cohort, not regulated utilities. Live multiples (verified June 2026): Constellation's forward price/earnings is about 25 times (GuruFocus 25.12 as of 2026-05-27, 49% above the IPP industry median of about 16.8 times), forward enterprise value to EBITDA about 13 times, forward free-cash-flow yield about 3.8%, and net debt about $5.9B base (pre- and early-Calpine). Vistra (VST): forward price/earnings about 18 times, EV/EBITDA about 10 times, free-cash-flow yield about 7.2%, net debt about $19.6B (with a fiscal-2026 adjusted-EBITDA guide of $6.8-7.6B) — cheaper and more cash-generative, but far more leveraged and gas-weighted, and less of a pure nuclear-scarcity story. Talen (TLN) and NRG are the other comparables (the Talen/Susquehanna co-location interconnection-service agreement is the key behind-the-meter regulatory precedent for the whole group); live multiples for Talen and NRG could not be cleanly retrieved this session — flagged as a gap. Ranking within the scale-out power-generation layer: Constellation is number one on AI-spending exposure, quality and nuclear-scarcity moat, and number one on balance-sheet conservatism, but last on valuation (the richest multiple) and on free-cash-flow yield (the lowest). It is the highest-quality, most-discovered, most-expensive name in the basket — you pay up for the best asset.
role_in_scaleout: Constellation is the prime firm-power generator for the build-out: it owns the scarce, NRC-licensed nuclear baseload (plus, after Calpine, on-demand gas) that lets it sell '24/7 firm' carbon-free power directly to hyperscalers on 20-year power-purchase agreements — the literal energy a data center must have before any chip, optic, or transformer matters. Demand is highly durable (20-year contracts, irreplaceable licensed assets, a tightening PJM capacity market) for as long as AI capital spending itself persists; the asset's terminal value is not at risk, only its premium multiple.
consensus_delta: There is no favorable place where we disagree with the market here — the street is largely right and already positioned. Consensus already prices Constellation as a premium AI-infrastructure asset (about 25 times forward, 49% above IPP peers, about 3.8% free-cash-flow yield); the flagship PPAs and the Calpine accretion are in every model. If anything the contrarian read cuts against the bull: the gap the market may be under-appreciating is on the risk side — that trailing free cash flow (about $1.3B in fiscal 2025) and a falling fiscal-2025 net margin (down 6.8 percentage points to 9.1%) sit far below a narrative that has fully discounted 2028-2029 cash. So our edge versus consensus is negative to neutral: you are buying a correctly-identified great asset at a price that already embeds the optionality, with the residual surprise risk skewed toward disappointment in AI-spending durability rather than upside. The one genuine non-consensus lever is the tightening PJM capacity market as a second earnings engine independent of any single PPA — but that, too, is increasingly in sell-side notes.
valley_of_death: The near-term (3-9 month) risks during the risky window just ahead are not about the balance sheet — Constellation is investment-grade with deal headroom — but about the multiple and sentiment. (1) An air pocket in AI spending: a single hyperscaler capital-spending guide-down or an AI-monetization scare (the market regime's named tripwire, with AI capital spending making up about 40% of 2026 S&P 500 earnings growth) would reduce risk exposure across the entire scale-out cohort and compress Constellation's premium multiple fastest, since its valuation is the most stretched. (2) Merchant-margin volatility: the first quarter of 2026 already showed zero-emission-credit (ZEC) pricing drag, extra nuclear refueling-outage days, and Winter Storm Fern cost-to-serve — outage and weather noise can pressure a quarter and rattle a richly-priced stock. (3) Calpine integration slippage or a gas-EBITDA re-rating (the market assigning fossil generation a lower multiple) would mechanically lower the sum-of-the-parts. (4) A behind-the-meter, co-location regulatory reversal at FERC (the unresolved Talen interconnection-service-agreement framework) would cloud the direct-to-data-center PPA model. None of these impairs terminal value, but each could drive a 15-25% drawdown from a 49%-premium starting multiple before any catalyst pays off.
positioning_washout: There is no classic forced-seller or yield-base-liquidation dynamic — Constellation is a growth and AI-momentum holding, not a high-yield name, so there is no dividend-cut-driven mechanical exit. The forced-selling pressure runs the opposite way: Constellation is a crowded, momentum-and-passive-heavy long inside the AI capital-spending theme (the regime flags BofA's fund-manager-survey cash at 3.9%, a sell signal, and AI capital spending as the single dominant factor in the current tape). A risk-reduction event would hit Constellation through factor-unwind and momentum-reversal selling, not a yield rotation — that is, it sits on the wrong side of a forced-selling washout, a candidate to be sold in a flush, which is exactly when its risk/reward would improve. Wait for that flush rather than buy the crowd.
forcing_function: The catalysts that force the re-rating are mostly behind us (Calpine close 2026-01-07; Meta/Clinton signing 2025-06-03; FERC Crane waiver 2026-06-01). The forward ones are: (a) announcement or signing of the 'late-innings' third hyperscaler PPA (undated — this would extend the flywheel and is the main positive surprise left); (b) the Crane Clean Energy Center restart on schedule around 2028 (the binding execution proof of the Microsoft deal); (c) the Meta/Clinton PPA commencement in June 2027 (the cash-flow turn-on); and (d) the Clinton early-site-permit decision before its 2027-03-15 expiry (small-modular-reactor optionality). Most are dated to 2027-2028 — too far out to anchor a near-term entry — and the nearest (the third PPA) is unverifiable and undated. There is no imminent, dated re-rating catalyst that is not already priced.
moderator_conclusion: Constellation is the highest-quality asset in the power-generation group and arguably the cleanest firm-power toll on the entire AI build-out — a genuinely wide, durable moat built on regulatory licenses and scale-cost advantage (32.4 GW of irreplaceable licensed nuclear), executing a transformative-but-accretive Calpine deal cleanly, with two proven 20-year hyperscaler PPAs and a tightening capacity market as a second earnings lever. The deep dive on management and the setup, with its strengthening, credibility-4 read, is correct on the business. But this is an investment-committee capital-allocation call, not a quality contest, and the market-regime lens is decisive: the load-bearing catalysts (Calpine close, Meta/Clinton, Microsoft/Crane) have already fired and the re-rating is spent; the stock trades at about 25 times forward (49% above IPP peers) with a roughly 3.8% free-cash-flow yield, and trailing free cash flow (about $1.3B) and falling fiscal-2025 margins sit far below a narrative that has fully discounted 2028-2029 cash. My reconciled base-case sum-of-the-parts is about $260, roughly 5% below the roughly $274 spot, with even-to-negative risk/reward (24% to the bull, 25% to the bear). Critically, Constellation fails the regime's test of resolving on its own drivers harder than anything else in the basket — its case rides the identical AI-spending-durability factor as every other scale-out name, so it adds concentration, not diversification, and it sits on the wrong side of a forced-selling washout. The regime explicitly tells me to penalize already-fired, already-run catalysts and to prize independence — both cut against Constellation here. Verdict: pass for now, conviction 2 — pass at this price; this is a watch-for-capitulation name, not an entry. Buy the asset on an AI-spending flush toward the $205-230 zone, where the premium compresses and the sum-of-the-parts gives real margin of safety. Separately, value conviction 3: as a pure value-versus-fair-price call stripped of the catalyst overlay, Constellation is fairly to slightly richly priced on a wide, non-eroding moat — not a value trap (the terminal asset is durable) but not cheap either, so a neutral 3, capped there by the full multiple rather than by any moat erosion.
interrogator_dossier:
# A deep dive on management and the setup — Constellation Energy

*Prepared for the investment committee. Capital is real. Data: live web (fiscal-2025 results, the first-quarter-2026 earnings call and release, the Calpine close, the hyperscaler PPA pipeline) as of 2026-06-22. Constellation trades on Nasdaq at about $274, with a market value of roughly $86-89B.*

## 1. Narrative arc
Constellation's story has gone through a deliberate, staged change from 'carbon-free merchant nuclear independent power producer with policy-protected cash flows' to 'the firm-power backbone of the AI build-out.' Phase 1 (2022-2023, just after the spin-off): decarbonization plus the Inflation Reduction Act's nuclear production-tax-credit floor, zero-emission-credit (ZEC) programs, and capital return — nuclear as a defensive clean-baseload asset. Phase 2 (2024, the pivot): the September-2024 Microsoft / Three Mile Island (Crane Clean Energy Center) 20-year PPA recast the thesis — nuclear became a scarce, around-the-clock firm power source that hyperscalers pay a structural premium for. Phase 3 (2025-2026, scale plus firming): the Meta/Clinton 20-year PPA (announced 2025-06-03, 1,121 MW, deliveries beginning June 2027) proved Microsoft was a template, not a one-off; and the $16.4B Calpine acquisition (closed 2026-01-07) bolts roughly 26 GW of gas plus the Geysers geothermal assets onto the 32.4 GW nuclear fleet (about 55 GW total), enabling a 24/7 firm product that blends carbon-free baseload with gas peaking. Drivers introduced: the data-center PPA flywheel, the co-location premium, small-modular-reactor optionality, and gas dispatchability. Drivers de-emphasized: pure decarbonization and ESG framing, and reliance on zero-emission credits (ZEC pricing is now a headwind line-item being replaced by long-dated PPAs at better economics).

## 2. Claims versus financials
Calpine accretion: first-quarter-2026 adjusted earnings of $2.74 per share versus $2.14 a year earlier; management attributes about $2 per share of full-year accretion; the fiscal-2026 guide of $11-12 versus fiscal-2025 of about $9.05-9.45 — corroborated; the roughly 20-30% earnings step-change is real and Calpine-driven. Revenue scaling: first-quarter-2026 revenue of $11.1B (up 64% year over year), fiscal-2025 revenue of $25.5B (up 8.3%) — true but driven by mix and volume (consolidating Calpine), not organic price. Free cash flow: only about $1.3B in fiscal 2025, but the guide is $8.4B combined for 2026-27 and $11.5-13B for 2028-29 — a tension: the trailing free-cash-flow yield is below 2% on a roughly $86B market value; the cash thesis is forward-loaded and unproven. Balance sheet: net debt of about $5.9B base versus Vistra's roughly $19.6B; Calpine added more than $10B (enterprise value of $26.6B including debt) — manageable off a conservative base, and investment-grade. Capital return: 1.2M shares bought at about $285, $335M since the last call, with a growing dividend — corroborated but small (about 0.4% annualized), not the thesis. Demand: 2026 hyperscaler spend about 75% higher than last year, about 5,000 MW into the PJM queue — corroborated by outside AI-spending data. The story is richer than the trailing numbers: fiscal-2025 net margin fell about 6.8 percentage points to 9.1%; the valuation prices the forward cash machine, not the trailing one.

## 3. Tone and confidence trajectory
Rising and offensive. The shift is from defensive ('the policy floor protects us') to offensive ('we are the scarce asset; hyperscalers compete for our megawatts'). The chief executive's comment that a major data-center transaction was in the 'late innings' ('I wouldn't discuss it if I didn't think it was viable') signals negotiating from strength. Affirming — not cutting — the $11-12 guidance through a demanding integration quarter is a confidence tell.

## 4. Guidance credibility
High. Management affirmed the fiscal-2026 adjusted-earnings range of $11-12 in the first quarter of 2026 despite integration complexity, outage timing, and Winter Storm Fern drag, and still beat first-quarter consensus ($2.74 versus $2.56, up 7%). The fiscal-2025 guide was narrowed and delivered. The multi-year free-cash-flow guide is specific and dated. Caveat: the forward free-cash-flow ramp embeds Calpine synergies plus PPA commencement (Meta in June 2027, Crane in 2028) that are not yet proven in cash. Reliability on what they control: high.

## 5. Analyst pressure points
'How much is already priced in?' (forward price/earnings of about 25 times versus an IPP median of about 17 times, 49% higher). Behind-the-meter, co-location regulatory risk (the Talen/Susquehanna interconnection-service-agreement precedent) — partly de-risked by the 2026-06-01 FERC PJM-grid-rule waiver, but the framework is still evolving. Calpine integration plus gas-fleet ESG dilution ('is this still a clean-energy premium asset?'). Timing gaps as the zero-emission credits roll off.

## 6. Red flags and green flags
Green: the affirmed guide plus the first-quarter beat through the Calpine close; two flagship 20-year PPAs prove repeatable premium demand, with a third in the 'late innings'; the scarcity moat (32.4 GW of nuclear that is irreplaceable on the AI timeline); and a conservative balance sheet versus peers. Red: thin trailing free cash flow (about $1.3B in fiscal 2025) against a roughly $86B market value; fiscal-2025 net margin down about 6.8 percentage points to 9.1%; a forward price/earnings of about 25 times (49% above the IPP median), leaving minimal margin of safety; and a single dominant shared driver — the same AI capital-spending wave as the whole basket, so low odds of resolving independently.

## 7. Hidden signals
The language migrated from 'zero-emission/carbon-free' to 'firm/24-7/reliable' — repositioning from an ESG asset to a grid-criticality asset (more durable, less policy-dependent). 'Capacity prices in PJM' is now a tailwind line — a second independent earnings lever as demand outgrows supply. Calpine gas was recoded from 'fossil liability' to 'the on-demand partner that makes nuclear sellable as firm power' (a sum-of-the-parts risk that cuts both ways). The small-modular/advanced reactor at Clinton is optionality, not committed capital (the early site permit expires 2027-03-15) — a free call management keeps alive.

## 8. Capital-allocation verdict
Trajectory: turning stronger — an improving franchise with repeatable premium PPAs, a transformative-but-accretive deal executed cleanly, affirmed guidance, and a tightening capacity market as a second lever; capital allocated offensively from scarcity-driven pricing power. The single thing to weigh before sizing: how much of the AI-power optionality is already in the roughly 25-times forward multiple (49% above IPP peers). Constellation is the highest-quality name in power generation but among the most already-discovered — the stock has run, the flagship catalysts are largely fired, and the forward free-cash-flow inflection that justifies the multiple is real but future-dated (2027-2029). This is a quality-compounder-at-full-price problem, not a mispricing. Moat: regulatory-license plus scale-cost — 32.4 GW of irreplaceable NRC-licensed baseload that no competitor can replicate inside the AI window — structurally wide; but the returns evidence is mixed (return on invested capital of about 15.5% is good, yet fiscal-2025 net margin fell about 6.8 percentage points and trailing free cash flow is thin), so the realized-return trend is stable to improving on guidance, not yet visibly widening in the financials. The secular force is a tailwind (AI-driven electrification), not a terminal threat; the structural risk is the inverse of most names — not demand erosion but demand-durability dependence: if AI capital spending digests, the premium multiple compresses faster than the asset base, but the terminal value of the licensed fleet is not impaired. Secular threat: manageable.

Management credibility 4 of 5; trajectory strengthening; wide moat; moat trend stable; secular threat manageable.
skeptic_kill_fact: There is no single fact that breaks the case: every load-bearing figure independently verifies against primary and investor-relations sources, so the bear case (pass / watch-for-capitulation) survives the kill-check rather than being broken. The only thing that could not be confirmed is the bull-side 'third hyperscaler PPA in late innings' — undated, unnamed, with no primary announcement — which is correctly treated as a soft, non-binding catalyst and is not load-bearing for the bear case.
skeptic_corrections: (1) Market value understated: the debate says about $86B; the live FMP quote shows about $98.4B at the $274.06 spot (share count is higher after the Calpine stock consideration). This makes the trailing free-cash-flow yield even thinner (about $1.29B / about $98B = about 1.3%), strengthening, not weakening, the bear. (2) Net debt base of about $5.26B per the fiscal-2025 10-K (the debate said about $5.9B) — immaterial, slightly conservative. (3) The FERC Crane/Three Mile Island waiver was granted around 2026-06-04 (a transfer of 760 MW of interconnection rights from the retiring Eddystone plant), not 2026-06-01 — a roughly 3-day date imprecision, immaterial; the substance (waiver granted, restart targeted for the second half of 2027, $1B Department of Energy loan in November 2025) confirms. (4) The Crane restart target is the second half of 2027 (835 MW), not 'about 2028' as stated in two places in the debate — a modest timeline correction that does not change the cash-is-forward-loaded conclusion. (5) Technical context the debate omits: the $274.06 spot sits below the 50-day moving average (about $284) and the 200-day moving average (about $319) and is about 34% off the 52-week high of $412.70 — in other words, the premium compression and de-rating the bear warns about is already under way, reinforcing the wait-for-the-flush stance.
skeptic_evidence: Constellation Energy IR, 'Constellation, Meta Sign 20-Year Deal for Clean, Reliable Nuclear Energy in Illinois' (2025-06-03): 1,121 MW Clinton Clean Energy Center 20-yr PPA, deliveries commence June 2027 — load-bearing PPA #2 CONFIRMED (investors.constellationenergy.com).

Hart Energy / Industrial Info, 'Constellation Completes $16.4B Calpine Deal' (closed 2026-01-07; ~$26.6B total incl ~$12.7B assumed Calpine net debt, ~26 GW gas + Geysers geothermal) — Calpine close CONFIRMED.

Utility Dive, 'Constellation's Three Mile Island nuclear restart gets boost with FERC waiver' (2026-06-04): FERC granted transfer of ~760 MW interconnection rights from retiring Eddystone to Crane; restart targeted H2-2027; backed by $1B DOE loan guarantee (Nov-2025) and 20-yr Microsoft PPA — Crane catalyst CONFIRMED (date corrected to 06-04).

CEG FY2025 results (SEC 8-K / company release): revenue $25.5B (+8%), GAAP net income $2.32B (FELL YoY on MtM/decommissioning), adj EPS $9.39, CFO $4.24B less capex $2.95B = FCF ~$1.29B, net debt ~$5.26B — confirms thin trailing FCF / falling-margin bear core. Live FMP quote 2026-06-18: CEG $274.06, mkt cap ~$98.4B, 50-DMA $284, 200-DMA $319, 52-wk high $412.70 (de-rating already underway).

scale.role: Single best-positioned firm-power supplier — 32.4 GW NRC-licensed nuclear, Meta/Clinton + Microsoft/Crane PPAs, Calpine closed.
scale.rationale: Irreplaceable scarcity (already-built licensed nuclear, the scarcest asset in the layer) and the purest nuclear-IPP toll after VST — a true CORE franchise (~85). Skeptic verdict CONFIRMED_WITH_CORRECTIONS, corrections are footnotes that REINFORCE the wait-stance (mcap higher so FCF yield thinner; de-rating already underway, ~34% off the high, below 50/200-DMA), so a ~8pt haircut. Catalyst FIRED (all three load-bearing events printed), base ~$260 ~5% below spot — seat-on-weakness, the least-redundant nuclear name after VST.
scale.demand_durability: Very high — licensed nuclear under long PPAs; the scarcest asset in the layer.
